dialogue_tree.title: Module 10: Planning & Implementation Intentions
dialogue_tree.learning_objective: Check readiness before moving to planning; Elicit the patient's ideas for plans first; Use if-then implementation intentions; Link plans to existing routines and check confidence
dialogue_tree.description: Learn to help patients create specific, realistic action plans using implementation intentions

nodes.patient_statement: I've been thinking about it more, and I think I'm ready to start making some changes with my eating and activity. I want to do something.

practitioner_choices.text: Great! Here's what you need to do: eat 1500 calories a day and exercise for 45 minutes, five times a week.
practitioner_choices.feedback: This jumps immediately to a practitioner-generated plan without exploring the patient's ideas.

practitioner_choices.text: It sounds like you're feeling ready to take some steps. Would you like to think about what a realistic first step might look like for you?
practitioner_choices.feedback: Perfect - acknowledges readiness, asks permission, and invites patient's ideas.

practitioner_choices.text: You say you're ready, but are you really? You've said that before.
practitioner_choices.feedback: This undermines the patient's expressed readiness rather than supporting it.

nodes.patient_statement: That sounds really intense. I don't think I can do all that right away.

practitioner_choices.text: If you're serious about losing weight, this is what it takes. You have to commit.
practitioner_choices.feedback: This creates pressure and treats planning as fixed rather than collaborative.

practitioner_choices.text: That does sound like a lot. What would feel like a realistic place for you to start?
practitioner_choices.feedback: Good recovery - validates concern and returns to patient-generated planning.

nodes.patient_statement: Maybe I could start by swapping my afternoon snack - I usually have chips - for something healthier? And maybe try to walk for 10 minutes a few times a week?

practitioner_choices.text: Those are thoughtful, specific ideas that you came up with. Let's get more specific - would it be okay to think about when and how these would happen?
practitioner_choices.feedback: Excellent - affirms patient's ideas and seeks permission to make plans concrete.

practitioner_choices.text: That's a good start, but you'll need to do more than that to see real results.
practitioner_choices.feedback: This devalues what the patient proposed as insufficient.

practitioner_choices.text: Let's make it every day, not just a few times a week. You should commit to daily walking.
practitioner_choices.feedback: This overrides the patient's realistic assessment of what they can commit to.

nodes.patient_statement: Well, when you put it like that, I'm not sure I'm ready after all.
nodes.patient_context: Patient pulls back after being doubted or pressured

practitioner_choices.text: See, I knew you weren't really ready. Let's just wait until you're more motivated.
practitioner_choices.feedback: This damages the relationship and abandons the planning process.

practitioner_choices.text: It sounds like I pushed too hard there. What would feel right for you to try?
practitioner_choices.feedback: Good - practitioner takes responsibility for the discord and returns to collaboration.

nodes.patient_statement: Sure, that would help. I usually snack around 3pm at my desk when I hit that afternoon slump.
nodes.patient_context: Patient identifies specific context for change

practitioner_choices.text: So if it's 3pm and you're at your desk feeling that slump, what could you do instead of reaching for chips?
practitioner_choices.feedback: Perfect - uses if-then format to create a concrete implementation intention.

practitioner_choices.text: You should keep healthy snacks at your desk. I recommend carrot sticks or almonds.
practitioner_choices.feedback: This gives advice without eliciting the patient's own ideas first.

nodes.patient_statement: I could grab the apple I bring for lunch but never eat? Or maybe take a quick walk around the office instead of snacking?

practitioner_choices.text: Those are both smart alternatives - using something you already have, or movement instead of food. Which feels most doable to you?
practitioner_choices.feedback: Good - affirms both options and lets patient choose what fits best.

practitioner_choices.text: Walking is better than the apple because you need the exercise more than changing your snack.
practitioner_choices.feedback: This takes over decision-making instead of letting the patient choose.

nodes.patient_statement: I think the apple. The walk might be too much to add right now, but eating the apple I already have - that seems easy.

practitioner_choices.text: So your plan is: if it's 3pm and I'm at my desk feeling the afternoon slump, then I'll eat my apple instead of chips. How confident do you feel about doing that?
practitioner_choices.feedback: Excellent - restates the if-then plan clearly and checks confidence before finalizing.

practitioner_choices.text: Good. Now let's plan your walking schedule in detail too.
practitioner_choices.feedback: This moves to another change without completing the first or checking if patient wants to continue.

nodes.patient_statement: Pretty confident, maybe an 8. I always have an apple with me, and my desk is private so no one will notice.
nodes.patient_context: Patient reports high confidence in plan

practitioner_choices.text: An 8 is solid. What would make it easier to remember to do this, especially on tough days?
practitioner_choices.feedback: Good - reinforces confidence and prepares for challenges.

practitioner_choices.text: Perfect! So you'll do this every day without fail starting tomorrow.
practitioner_choices.feedback: This makes the plan inflexible and pressures absolute adherence.

nodes.patient_statement: Maybe I could put the apple right on my keyboard in the morning? That way I'd see it and remember. And if I have a really bad day and still want chips, that's okay - I can try again the next day.
nodes.patient_context: Patient plans reminder and flexible approach

practitioner_choices.text: You're thinking ahead about both a reminder and how to be flexible with yourself. That's a really thoughtful approach. What about the walking - did you want to make a plan for that too, or start with just the snack swap?
practitioner_choices.feedback: Perfect - affirms planning and lets patient decide scope without pressure.

practitioner_choices.text: No, you can't have chips even on bad days. You have to stick to the plan.
practitioner_choices.feedback: This creates unrealistic expectations and treats plans as fixed.

nodes.patient_statement: Let me try the snack thing for a couple weeks and see how it goes. Once that's a habit, I'll think about adding the walking. One thing at a time feels more realistic.
nodes.outcome: Good engagement - Through patient-led planning with clear implementation intentions and confidence checks, a realistic and achievable plan emerged.
nodes.learning_summary: Effective planning in MI involves: (1) confirming readiness before planning, (2) eliciting the patient's own ideas first, (3) creating specific if-then implementation intentions, (4) linking to existing routines, and (5) checking confidence. Plans should be patient-generated, realistic, and flexible.

nodes.patient_statement: This all feels like too much pressure. I don't think I'm ready for this after all.
nodes.patient_context: Poor outcome from directive or rigid planning
nodes.outcome: Poor engagement - Imposing plans, pressuring commitment, or creating rigid rules led to the patient withdrawing from the change process.
nodes.learning_summary: Imposing practitioner-generated plans, pressuring for commitment, creating rigid rules, and overloading with multiple changes all undermine the planning process. Effective MI planning is collaborative, patient-generated, confidence-appropriate, and allows for flexibility.
